claim: Bariatric surgery reduces resolution of diabetes.

result.claim: Bariatric surgery reduces resolution of diabetes.
result.reasoning:
The claim that bariatric surgery reduces the resolution of diabetes is strongly refuted by multiple high-credibility studies. A randomized controlled trial (RCT) with a credibility rating of 4.3/5 and a sample size of 262, with follow-up up to 12 years (Paper ID: 466f1a5333f958f73cfdeafae141952da3053b25), explicitly states that individuals undergoing bariatric surgery had "higher rates of diabetes remission compared with medical/lifestyle intervention after 7 to 12 years of follow-up" (prop_42446). This study also reported a "12.7% diabetes remission rate at 12 years" in the bariatric surgery group (prop_42439) and "superior glycemic control" (prop_42444) with "less diabetes medication use" (prop_42445, prop_42437). Furthermore, a systematic review from 2024 (Paper ID: c71c0b6cfd0ca942e401882d3dab969cf82376a7) concludes that "Bariatric surgery has been shown to significantly affect type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) remission, particularly in obese individuals" (chunk_5509) and is a "highly effective intervention for individuals with T2DM" (chunk_5510). No evidence was found to support the claim that bariatric surgery reduces diabetes resolution.

**Evidence Note:** 466f1a5333f958f73cfdeafae141952da3053b25, c71c0b6cfd0ca942e401882d3dab969cf82376a7

evidence.text: Metabolic and bariatric surgery (MBS) is the most effective long-term intervention for obesity.
